refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the acceptance criterion (≥0.999), specifies the measurement scope (bidirectional link), and defines the evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to equipment immobilization or unsafe states.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ customer-defined target (default 0.999). Verification method: automated telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single communication link with best-effort reliability (no redundancy)
DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-hop redundancy and dynamic routing
DP-D: Hybrid approach: primary cellular + secondary satellite link with intelligent link selection
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Dual independent communication paths (primary + secondary failover) with automatic switchover logic and continuous link health monitoring.

potential_sources: Link health monitoring logic couples to both primary and secondary paths; must be decoupled via standardized link status interface.
Failover decision logic couples to network configuration; mitigated by parameterized failover thresholds.
Safe state transition (FR-NGHE-010.2) couples to vehicle control; addressed via SafetyAlert interface.
coupling_check.notes: DP-B exhibits acceptable coupling. The dual-path architecture decouples physical link implementation from failover logic via standardized health monitoring. Failover thresholds are parameterizable, allowing tuning without redesign. Coupling to safety systems is managed through explicit SafetyAlert signaling.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Two independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + private radio) are available at the worksite.
ASSUMPTION: Link health can be reliably detected via periodic heartbeat or explicit status messages.
RISK: If both paths fail simultaneously (common-mode failure), availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: diversity in link technology and routing.

RISK: Failover latency may exceed safe state transition time. Mitigation: pre-position safe state logic on vehicle; link loss triggers immediate safe state without waiting for tele-operator acknowledgment.
RISK: Link availability measurement methodology must be agreed with customer; ambiguity in 'evaluation period' or 'link operational' definition could invalidate acceptance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-B to explicitly specify: (1) link health detection mechanism (heartbeat interval, timeout threshold), (2) failover decision criteria and latency budget, (3) safe state transition trigger (link loss vs. explicit command), (4) availability measurement protocol (continuous logging, audit trail). Consider adding tertiary link (satellite) if dual-path availability target cannot be met via analysis.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-path communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and telemetry/tasking routing. Responsible for maintaining the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link and signaling link state to Safety Services and Vehicle control systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, etc.) from tele-operator via primary or secondary link; routed to Vehicle module.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state, sensor data, and link health status back to tele-operator and monitoring systems via primary or secondary link.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link loss or degradation to Safety Services module to trigger safe state transition on Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts the underlying communication infrastructure (primary and secondary paths); Teleoperation module manages link selection, failover, and health monitoring at this interface.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Dual-path management, failover, and link health monitoring are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert signaling requires coordination with Safety Services, but this is a clean interface boundary.

Safety Services owns safe state transition logic, not link availability. Assigning link management to Safety Services would couple communication infrastructure to safety logic, violating separation of concerns. Safety Services should react to SafetyAlert from Teleoperation, not own the link itself.

Vehicle owns vehicle control and actuation, not communication infrastructure. Assigning link availability to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across the system and create tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and network management.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. While it could theoretically own link availability, this would bypass the Teleoperation module and prevent reuse of teleoperation logic across multiple vehicle types or configurations.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of link availability because: (1) it owns the communication paths and link health monitoring logic, (2) it interfaces with both operator (Tasking in) and vehicle (Telemetry out, SafetyAlert out), (3) it can manage failover transparently without exposing link details to Vehicle or Safety Services, (4) it maintains a clean separation of concerns: Teleoperation handles 'how to communicate reliably,' Safety Services handles 'what to do if communication fails.'
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module is not equipped with dual-path hardware/firmware, link availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: ensure Teleoperation IM includes or interfaces with dual-path communication hardware.

RISK: SafetyAlert interface may introduce latency in safe state transition if Teleoperation-to-Safety Services signaling is slow. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert as a high-priority, low-latency interface; consider hardware-level link loss detection that triggers safe state without software mediation.

RISK: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if it must process all telemetry and tasking. Mitigation: use message queuing and asynchronous processing; ensure failover logic does not block command/telemetry flow.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Teleoperation module includes or has direct access to dual-path communication hardware (e.g., cellular modem + private radio transceiver). Define the SafetyAlert interface with explicit latency and reliability requirements (e.g., link loss detected and SafetyAlert sent within 100 ms). Specify link health monitoring as a background task that does not block command/telemetry processing.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (dual independent paths with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve ≥0.999 link availability). The DP is logically sound: redundancy is a proven approach to availability. Coupling is acceptable and managed via standardized interfaces. Assumptions (two independent paths available, link health detectable) are reasonable for a modern heavy equipment system. Risks (common-mode failure, failover latency) are identified and mitigatable. DP-B is ready for IM assignment.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic must not diffuse across Teleoperation and Vehicle modules. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns failover; Vehicle reacts only to SafetyAlert.
Link health monitoring must not diffuse across Teleoperation and Safety Services. Mitigation: Teleoperation monitors link health; Safety Services monitors safe state.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is a strong containment fit (score 0.95). The module owns communication infrastructure and can implement dual-path management, failover, and link health monitoring without diffusion. SafetyAlert interface cleanly separates link availability (Teleoperation) from safe state transition (Safety Services). No architectural diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of link availability is feasible: Teleoperation module can log link state transitions and compute availability as (time_up) / (total_time). Acceptance criterion (≥0.999) is measurable. Evaluation period must be agreed with customer (e.g., 30 days of continuous operation). Verification method: automated telemetry logging + post-evaluation statistical analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for achieving ≥0.999 link availability. Sub-FRs (link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, audit logging) are allocated to Teleoperation and Safety Services. Ownership boundaries are explicit.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-B to specify link health detection mechanism (heartbeat interval, timeout threshold) and failover latency budget.
Define SafetyAlert interface with explicit latency and reliability requirements (e.g., link loss detected and SafetyAlert sent within 100 ms).
Confirm that Teleoperation module includes or has direct access to dual-path communication hardware.
Agree with customer on availability measurement protocol and evaluation period (e.g., 30 days of continuous operation, link operational = bidirectional heartbeat received within 5 seconds).

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs: link loss detection, safe state transition, redundant paths, and audit logging. Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. DP-B is sound and ready for decomposition. Teleoperation module is the correct owner. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is justified to detail link health monitoring, failover logic, and safe state transition mechanisms.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall initiate safe state transition upon link loss.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall maintain redundant communication paths to achieve target availability.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.

assumptions: Two independent communication paths (e.g., cellular + private radio) are available at the worksite.
Link health can be reliably detected via periodic heartbeat or explicit status messages.
Safe state transition can be triggered by link loss without waiting for tele-operator acknowledgment.
Availability measurement is based on continuous logging of link state transitions over the agreed evaluation period.
Customer will define the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days) and link operational criteria (e.g., bidirectional heartbeat within 5 seconds).
open_risks: Common-mode failure: both communication paths fail simultaneously. Mitigation: diversity in link technology and routing; consider tertiary link (satellite).
Failover latency exceeds safe state transition time. Mitigation: pre-position safe state logic on vehicle; link loss triggers immediate safe state without waiting for tele-operator acknowledgment.
Link availability measurement methodology ambiguity. Mitigation: agree with customer on evaluation period, link operational definition, and measurement protocol before acceptance testing.
Teleoperation module may not include dual-path hardware. Mitigation: confirm hardware availability during detailed design phase.
evidence_needed: Availability analysis: calculate expected availability of dual-path architecture given individual path failure rates and failover latency.
Link health detection specification: define heartbeat interval, timeout threshold, and detection latency.
Failover logic specification: define failover decision criteria, latency budget, and safe state transition trigger.
SafetyAlert interface specification: define latency and reliability requirements.
Customer agreement on availability measurement protocol and evaluation period.
Confirmation of dual-path communication hardware availability and integration plan.
